coredata.dc:title: A lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array for simultaneous pattern recognition of multiple antibiotics
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Yaning
Cui, Shengnan
Li, Wentao
Fan, Xiaojian
Liu, Yuanyuan
Xu, Shenghao
Luo, Xiliang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CDs) have gained extremely consideration in recent years as a result of their predominant fluorescence properties. Combining these unique advantages, herein, a “lab-on-a-carbon nanodot” based cross-reactive sensor array for multiple antibiotics discrimination was introduced. Four types of CDs which were synthesized by simply mixing diphosphorus pentoxide, different amino acids (isoleucine, leucine and histidine) and water, were used as sensing receptors. Eight antibiotics can be well distinguished on account of the different fluorescence responses by linear discrimination analysis (LDA). Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) results indicated that the main interaction force between CDs and antibiotics are hydrogen bonding and van der waals forces. More importantly, binary and ternary antibiotics mixtures can also be adequately recognized in real samples such as human urine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensor array
Carbon nanodots
Antibiotics
Lab-on-a-carbon nanodot
Statistical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of four types of CDs
Optical properties and morphology characterization of these CDs
Principle of the lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of one-pot synthesis of the CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of these four types of CDs. Inset shows the pictures of these four types of CDs under 365 nm UV light illumination.
Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) CDs-1, (B) CDs-2, (C) CDs-3 and (D) CDs-4 at excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 460 nm.
(A) TEM images of these CDs. (B) AFM images of these CDs.

(A) Schematic illustration of antibiotic recognition on the basis of the lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array. (B) Fluorescence response of these four CDs (1/20) against various antibiotics (250 μM); (C) LDA plot for the discrimination result with 95% confidence ellipses. (Fluorescence changes were directly obtained by using gel bioimaging system).

(A) Discrimination result of fluorescence response against different concentrations of OTC (100, 200, 500, 5000 and 10,000 nM). (B) Plot of discriminant factor (1) vs. the OTC concentration. (C) Discrimination result of the fluorescence response for two or three antibiotics mixtures. CDs used here were the CDs (1/20).
(A) XPS spectrum of C1s for CDs-1(black line), CDs-1+OTC (red line) and CDs-1+NEO (blue line). (B) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs-1(black line), CDs-1+OTC (red line) and CDs-1+NEO (blue line).

(A) Discrimination result of eight antibiotics (250 μM) in the presence of human urine. (B) Discrimination result of binary or ternary antibiotics mixtures in the presence of human urine. CDs used here were the CDs (1/20).

head.ce:title: A lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array for simultaneous pattern recognition of multiple antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE; College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Breast Surgery, The Eighth Peoples’ Hospital of Qingdao, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Eighth Peoples’ Hospital of Qingdao

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Breast Surgery, The Eighth Peoples’ Hospital of Qingdao, Qingdao 266100, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A “lab-on-a-carbon nanodot” based sensor array was designed for simultaneous analysis of multiple antibiotics. Based on this, single antibiotic, binary or ternary antibiotics mixtures can both be effectively identified and differentiated even in real samples such as human urine.

Carbon nanodots (CDs) have gained extremely consideration in recent years as a result of their predominant fluorescence properties. Combining these unique advantages, herein, a “lab-on-a-carbon nanodot” based cross-reactive sensor array for multiple antibiotics discrimination was introduced. Four types of CDs which were synthesized by simply mixing diphosphorus pentoxide, different amino acids (isoleucine, leucine and histidine) and water, were used as sensing receptors. Eight antibiotics can be well distinguished on account of the different fluorescence responses by linear discrimination analysis (LDA). Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) results indicated that the main interaction force between CDs and antibiotics are hydrogen bonding and van der waals forces. More importantly, binary and ternary antibiotics mixtures can also be adequately recognized in real samples such as human urine.

ce:section.ce:para: Simultaneous analysis of multiple antibiotics rather than individual antibiotic is of high significance because of the serious threats to public health caused by various drug residues or accumulation of antibiotics [1,2]. Until now, numerous analytical strategies have been proposed for analysis of multiple residual antibiotics, including surface enhanced raman scattering (SERS), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectra (HPLC-MS), fluorescent nanoclusters and electrochemical aptasensor [3–5]. Nevertheless, non-negligible drawbacks such as time consuming sample pretreatment, expensive instruments or the requirement of highly trained technical personnel usu ally restrict their regular environment and food safety monitoring [6]. As an alternative method for solving these shortcomings, fluorescence sensing techniques have attracted widespread attention due to their advantages of easy operation, high sensitivity and low cost. However, the traditional ‘lock-key’ fluorescence sensing mode requires specific fluorescent probe to recognize one antibiotic, which it is difficult to simultaneously identify multiple antibiotics with one approach [7,8]. Hence, it is highly desirable and challenging to develop more convenient and rapid methods for simultaneous analysis of multiple antibiotics.

As a burgeoning approach which can overcome such limitations, cross-reactive sensor array (chemical nose/tongue) has been increasingly fabricated for simultaneous analysis of multiple analytes [9–15]. Different from conventional “1 vs. 1” (one probe only for one analyte) principle, highly specific receptors are unnecessary for the “N vs. N” sensor arrays (multiple probes for multiple analytes) [16]. Their nonspecific sensing receptors could exhibit unique interactions with the given target analytes, giving a unique optical “fingerprint” for pattern identification [17]. So far, although various nonspecific sensing receptors have been elegantly designed, these materials suffer from obvious drawbacks such as sophisticated modification or high cost [18]. Therefore, the development of sensing receptors with simple design which do not require surface modification is highly desirable. As a newly emerged type of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon nanodots (CDs) have gained considerable attention because of their superior photostability, low/nontoxic toxicity and cost-effective synthesis [19–22]. The abundant surface functional groups of CDs could interact with various types of antibiotics and produce fingerprint-like response patterns [23]. With these advantages, CDs are considered to be promising candidates as nonspecific sensing receptors for fabricating cross-reactive sensor arrays, which had only been scarcely exploited [24].

Herein, a “lab-on-a-carbon nanodot” based sensor array was designed for simultaneous analysis of multiple antibiotics. Four types of CDs derived from different amino acids were selected as the nonspecific sensing receptors. Upon the addition of eight antibiotics, the fluorescence intensities of these four types of CDs altered in different degrees. Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) was used for dealing with the resulting response pattern data, to give a more intuitive identification clustering map. Based on this, eight antibiotics were effectively identified and differentiated. Moreover, the proposed “lab-on-a-carbon nanodot” based sensor array was also successfully applied for the discrimination of the binary or ternary mixtures of antibiotics in human urine samples.

All chemicals used in this experiment were in analytical grade. Isoleucine (Iso), leucine (Leu), histidine (His), tyrosine (Tyr), phosphorus pentoxide, streptomycin (SM), chloramphenicol (CHL), neomycin (NEO), aureomycin (CTC), erythromycin (ERY), Oxytetracycline (OTC), spiramycin (SPM) and tetracycline (TC) were obtained from Sigma (USA).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was accomplished on a JEM-2100 microscope. Fluorescence spectra were measured on a Hitach F-7000 spectrometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were accomplished with a tapping mode. X-ray photoelecgon spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a VG ESCALAB MKII spectrometer. FT-IR was accomplished on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. A tanon bioimaging system was used to obtain the fluorescence images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of four types of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 5.0 g of phosphorus pentoxide and 0.04 g of amino acid (Iso for CDs-1, Leu for CDs-2, His for CDs-3, Tyr for CDs-4) were mixed, and then 4.0 mL deionized water was rapidly added to the above mixture. Whereafter, another 16.0 mL of deionized water was added to the above mixture. In the end, CDs were obtained after centrifuging at 5000 rpm for 3 min.

First, four types of CDs (1/20) were obtained by diluting original CDs 20 times using PBS (pH = 7.4), respectively. Then, they were added to a 96-well plate with 200 μL for each hole, respectively. Whereafter, 5.0 μL of 10 mM different target antibiotics were added to each hole, respectively, and reacted for 1 h at room temperature. (original fluorescence data were shown in Table S1 in the supporting information) At last, bioimaging system was used for recording the fluorescent images. Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) was used to process the obtained fluorescent data in SPSS v16.0.

Antibiotic samples (10 mM) were obtained by diluting with morning urine at a ratio of 1:1 (v/v). Then, 5.0 μL of antibiotic samples were added to each hole of the 96-well plate, respectively. After reacting for 1 h at room temperature, bioimaging system was used for recording the fluorescent images. LDA was used to process the obtained fluorescent data in SPSS v16.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and morphology characterization of these CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and AFM were used to characterize the morphology of these four types of CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 3 A, these four types of CDs were all spherical and well dispersed from each other, with an average diameters of approximately 2.0 nm. From typical two-dimensional AFM images (Fig.3B), the spherical morphology and cross-sectional heights also demonstrate these four types of monodispersed CDs possess an average diameter of approximately 2.0 nm, which was in accordance with TEM results. Moreover, FT-IR spectroscopy was carried out to investigate the surface functional groups of these CDs owing to the high significance of the surface properties of these CDs in predicting the recognition capability of a sensor array. It can be seen from Fig. S1, the appearance of vibrational absorption band of CO (1645 cm−1), stretching vibrations of CH (2920 cm−1) and NH (3410 cm−1), asymmetric stretching vibrations of CNHC (1459 cm−1and 1225 cm−1)and bending vibrations of NH (702 cm−1) indicated that these four types of CDs mainly possessed two crucial functional groups (NH2 and COOH) on their surface [26]. Therefore, diverse binding affinities to different target antibiotics on the dasis of electrostatic attraction, hydrophilic interaction or hydrophobic may be existed from these functional groups, generating diverse unique “fingerprint” response for each antibiotic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: The design and assembly process of the lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array for multiple antibiotics recognition can be simply demonstrated in Fig. 4 A. Disparate fluorescence responses can be generated after adding diverse antibiotics to the same CDs. Meanwhile, different fluorescence responses can also be caused after adding the same antibiotic to different CDs. Accordingly, the discrimination or pattern recognition of multiple antibiotics can be achieved through statistical analysis of these different fluorescence responses by LDA.

To investigated the ability of the lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array for pattern recognition of multiple antibiotics, eight antibiotics, i.e., three tetracyclines (TC, OTC and CTC), two aminoglycosides (SM and NEO), one amide alcohols (CHL) and two macrolides (SPM and ERY) were selected for the discrimination examinations. As shown in Fig. 4B, the sensor array shows unique fluorescence recognition patterns (or “fingerprint maps”) to each of these antibiotics, indicating its feasibility for antibiotics identification. In order to give a more intuitive discrimination results, a visual two-dimensional (2D) plot under the 95% confidence ellipses was obtained by using LDA statistic analysis (Fig. 4C). Three replicates for each antibiotic was carried out, generating generating a matrix of 4 CDs × 8 antibiotics × 3 replicates. It can be seen from Fig. 4C, All target antibiotic were separated from each other without any overlap, demonstrating that they were effectively recognized by LDA, indicating the excellent ability of this lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array for multiple antibiotics discrimination. Moreover, we also used principal component analysis (PCA) to discriminate antibiotics and the result was shown in Fig.S2. As shown in Fig.S2, each antibiotic was separated well with each other, indicating PCA could also be used to discriminate antibiotics. It's worth noting that after adding eight antibiotics to the CDs respectively, fluorescence intensity of CDs will be changed. If the change of fluorescence intensity (I-I0) was obtained by fluorescence spectra, the enhancement or quenching of the fluorescent intensities were around 4˜35% of the original intensity (Fig. S3A). Although antibiotics can be well differentiated (Fig. S3B), the process of obtaining fluorescence changes is relatively complicated because fluorescence spectrum need to be measured one by one, which is not conducive to rapid detection. Therefore, the change of fluorescence intensity (I-I0) was directly obtained by photography with a gel bioimaging system instead of fluorescence spectra, which we learn from our previous works [9,16]. To obtain the fluorescence changes by this way, although the room for fluorescence changes is narrowed (from 4˜35% to 1˜8%), antibiotics can still be well differentiated from the experiment result (Fig. 4). More importantly, to obtain the fluorescence changes by using gel bioimaging system, the most obvious advantage is that the fluorescence changes of all samples can be obtained within few seconds at a time. High throughput fluorescence change data can be obtained, which is conducive to rapid detection. Therefore, quick and accurate antibiotics discrimination can be achieved by this proposed method. In brief, both LDA and PCA are acceptable for antibiotics discrimination in this experiment. The discrimination accuracy of 40 unknown samples at 250 μM was proved to be 100% (Tables S2). In addition, the reaction conditions of pH value (Fig. S4) and the incubation time (Fig. S5) were also optimized.

As a sensor array, discriminating different analytes at diverse concentration levels still remains a great challenge [27]. To investigate the probability of the lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array for antibiotic quantification, OTC at different concentrations were carried out for quantitative analysis as an example. Since Factor 2 in the LDA plots was only 2.8%, it is acceptable to employ Factor 1 (96.5%) to correlate the concentration of OTC. As shown in Fig. 5 A, antibiotics at nanomolar concentrations can be well discriminated by this sensitive lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array. Moreover, the linearity of the dose–response curve demonstrates that the interactions are not only homogeneous but also stable, revealing highly reproducible of this lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array (Fig. 5B). In addition, we also try to use this lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array to discriminate the two or three mixtures of antibiotics. The mixture antibiotics for discrimination in Fig. 5C were randomly selected two or three from these eight antibiotics. It can be seen from Fig. 5C, the two or three antibiotics mixtures can be well separated with each other without any overlap. This further revealed the proposed lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array had the potential ability to analyze complicated samples.

For the mechanism study, we studied it mainly from two aspects. As is reported, electrostatic attraction, hydrophobic interaction and hydrogen bonding are the main interact force between antibiotics and small molecules [28]. Firstly, isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) experiment was performed to explore the interaction between CDs and target antibiotics using OTC and CDs-1 as an example. The interact force can be roughly divided into three categories according to the thermodynamic parameters ΔH and ΔS [29]. In detail, when ΔS < 0 and ΔH < 0, van der waals forces and hydrogen are the main interact force. When ΔS > 0 and ΔH > 0, hydrophobic bond is the main interact force. When ΔS > 0 and ΔH ≈ 0, the main interact force was electrostatic interactions. In this experiment, the CDs interacted with antibiotics mainly by hydrogen bonding and van der waals forces because of both the negative ΔS and ΔH (Fig. S6).

Secondly, according to the previous reports, fluorescence could be enhanced with the prolonged fluorescence lifetime and decreased binding energy, while fluorescence could be reduced with the increasing binding energy and curtate fluorescence lifetime [30]. Therefore, fluorescence lifetime and XPS were carried out to investigate the changes of the CDs properties after incubation with target antibiotics. It can be seen from Fig. 6 , the fluorescence lifetime was prolonged while the binding energy of the C1s was decreased after adding OTC to the CDs-1. Nevertheless, the fluorescence lifetime was curtate while the binding energy of the C1s was increased after adding NEO to the CDs-1. Both the above results were in accordance with the decreased fluorescence after adding NEO and the enhanced fluorescence after adding OTC to the CDs-1. Therefore, we think that the change of fluorescence intensity resulted from the changed the fluorescence lifetime and binding energy of CDs. However, indepth mechanism study is still need in the future work.

In order to validate the application of the lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array in real samples, eight antibiotics diluted with human urine at a ratio of 1:1 (v/v) was discriminated. It can be seen from Fig. 7 A, eight antibiotics can still be well separated without any overlap even in the urine conditions. Moreover, eight randomly selected ternary or binary mixtures of antibiotics could also be well discriminated without overlap, demonstrating that the lab-on-a-carbon nanodot has feasible potential capacity for discriminating antibiotics in real biological samples (Fig. 7B).

In conclusion, a lab-on-a-carbon nanodot sensor array was designed and constructed for simultaneous discrimination of multiple antibiotics, which was proved to be excellent in the discrimination of eight antibiotics. Furthermore, this sensor array also exhibits high recognition accuracy and good anti-interference for discriminating ternary or binary mixtures of antibiotics in complex media like human urine samples. It is noteworthy that this research is considered as an elementary inquiry of manufacturing CDs-based sensor array systems. We expect that this work would establish a novel CDs-based antibiotics detection method with available potential applications in clinical diagnostics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (21675093, 21505081), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2019YQ13), the Doctoral Found of QUST (010022832) and the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province, China (ts20110829).
The authors have declared no conflict of interest

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.126694.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer-based cantilever array sensors for oxytetracycline detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Hou, X.J. Bai, C.Y. Xing, N.Y. Gu, B.L. Zhang, J.L. Tang, Aptamer-based cantilever array sensors for oxytetracycline detection, Anal. Chem. 85 (2013) 2010-2014.

Chen, Novel label-free and high-throughput microchip electrophoresis platform for multiplex antibiotic residues detection based on aptamer probes and target catalyzed hairpin assembly for signal amplification, Biosens. Bioelectron. 97 (2017) 100-106.

Liu, A visual application of gold nanoparticles: simple, reliable and sensitive detection of kanamycin based on hydrogen-bonding recognition, Sens. Actuators B: Chem. 243 (2017) 946-954.

Bioelectron. 97 (2017) 203-207.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.M. Freire, N.D.B. Le, Z.W. Jiang, C.S. Kim, V.M. Rotello, P.B.A. Fechine, NH2-rich Carbon Quantum Dots: A protein-responsive probe for detection and identification, Sens. Actuators B: Chem. 255 (2018) 2725-2732.

L. Pan, S. Sun, A. Zhang, K. Jiang, L. Zhang, C. Dong, Q. Huang, A. Wu, H. Lin, Adv. Mater. 27 (2015) 7782-7787.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence sensor array based on amino acid derived carbon dots for pattern-based detection of toxic metal ions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Histidine-derived nontoxic nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Huang, C. Li, S. Zhu, H. Wang, C. Chen, Z. Wang, T. Bai, Z. Shi, S. Feng, Histidine-derived nontoxic nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging applications, Langmuir 30 (2014) 13542-13548.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoparticle–fluorophore complexes: sensitive and discerning “noses” for biosystems sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U.H.F. Bunz, V.M. Rotello, Gold nanoparticle–fluorophore complexes: sensitive and discerning “noses” for biosystems sensing, Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 49 (2010) 3268-3279.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Berg, Modulation of protein–protein interactions with small organic molecules, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 42 (2003) 2462-2481.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermodynamics of protein association reactions: forces contributing to stability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.D. Ross, S. Subramanian, Thermodynamics of protein association reactions: forces contributing to stability, Biochemistry 20 (1981) 3096-3102.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of protein adsorption on the fluorescence of ultrasmall gold nanoclusters
